name: Vili
description: Vili, along with his brothers Odin and Vé, is one of the first generation of Norse gods, sons of Borr and the giantess Bestla. He is primarily known for his role in the creation of the world and humankind. After defeating the giant Ymir, Vili and his brothers fashioned the cosmos from Ymir's body. Vili bestowed upon humanity wit and feeling, contributing to their intellectual and emotional capacity. While Odin is often seen as the chief of the Aesir, Vili's contribution to creation is undeniable. He represents the power of will and the intellect, aspects essential for the development of both the world and its inhabitants. Vili's influence, while less explicitly detailed in surviving texts than Odin's, is crucial to understanding the foundation of Norse cosmology and the nature of humanity. He embodies inner strength, inspiration, and the capacity for creative thought. He and his brothers established the natural order, setting the sun and moon in their courses and dividing day from night. Vili's gift of wit and feeling to humankind is a significant aspect of his legacy, shaping the human experience and setting them apart from other creatures. Vili is a powerful, primordial deity whose actions laid the groundwork for the world and the human condition as understood in Norse mythology.
subtitle: Creator God of Wit and Feeling

keyMyths.title: The Slaying of Ymir
keyMyths.description: Vili, along with his brothers Odin and Vé, slew the giant Ymir. From Ymir's body, they created the world, shaping the land, sea, and sky.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Humans
keyMyths.description: After creating the world, Vili and his brothers found two logs on the shore. Odin gave them breath and life, Vili gave them wit and feeling, and Vé gave them form and senses.

keyMyths.title: Odin's Absence
keyMyths.description: In some accounts, when Odin was away, Vili and Vé shared his wife Frigg. This highlights their close relationship and potential shared authority.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A primary source for Norse mythology, including accounts of the creation of the world and humanity.
